description: Scoring rubric and synthesis rules for evaluating and combining subagent reports into a final recommendation.

phases.name: Validate Each Report
phases.description: Check each report for correctness independently before scoring. Do not just accept the subagent's reasoning.

checks.name: Complexity check
instructions: Verify the time/space claims independently. Is O(n log n) actually right, or did the subagent undercount a nested loop? Are amortized costs labeled correctly?
Flag any claims that seem off. Write a one-line note: 'Path 2 claims O(n) but the inner loop makes this O(n^2) in the worst case.'

checks.name: Walkthrough check
instructions: Trace the example yourself mentally. Does the algorithm produce the stated result?
If the walkthrough is vague or skips steps, note it.

checks.name: Edge case check
instructions: Are the listed edge cases genuine? Do the explanations hold up?
Is there a critical edge case the subagent missed? Add it to your analysis.

phases.name: Score Each Approach
phases.description: Score on dimensions most relevant to the problem. Always include correctness and complexity. Add domain-specific dimensions as appropriate.

scoringScale.disqualificationRule: A single Disqualifying score on any dimension eliminates an approach from being the primary recommendation (though it may still contribute to a hybrid).

alwaysRelevantDimensions.name: Correctness
alwaysRelevantDimensions.whatToAssess: Does the algorithm actually solve the problem? Any subtle bugs?

alwaysRelevantDimensions.name: Time complexity
alwaysRelevantDimensions.whatToAssess: Is it optimal or near-optimal for the constraints?

alwaysRelevantDimensions.name: Space complexity
alwaysRelevantDimensions.whatToAssess: Is memory usage acceptable? Any hidden allocations?

alwaysRelevantDimensions.name: Edge case robustness
alwaysRelevantDimensions.whatToAssess: How gracefully does it handle boundary inputs?

alwaysRelevantDimensions.name: Implementation difficulty
alwaysRelevantDimensions.whatToAssess: How easy is this to get right in real code?

conditionallyRelevantDimensions.name: Streaming/online capability
conditionallyRelevantDimensions.useWhen: Problem involves continuous or unbounded input

conditionallyRelevantDimensions.name: Parallelizability
conditionallyRelevantDimensions.useWhen: User mentions distributed or concurrent context

conditionallyRelevantDimensions.name: Mutability
conditionallyRelevantDimensions.useWhen: Input may change after initial processing

conditionallyRelevantDimensions.name: Constant factors
conditionallyRelevantDimensions.useWhen: Two approaches share asymptotic complexity but differ in practice

conditionallyRelevantDimensions.name: Code readability
conditionallyRelevantDimensions.useWhen: User's codebase or team context suggests this matters

phases.name: Check for Hybridization
phases.description: Before picking a winner, check whether combining approaches yields a genuinely better solution.

questions.question: Does Path A have the right algorithm but Path B handles edge cases better?
questions.action: Recommend Path A with Path B's edge case strategy incorporated.

questions.question: Does Path A excel at the common case and Path B at the degenerate case?
questions.action: Recommend Path A with a fallback to Path B's strategy under detected conditions.

questions.question: Do two paths decompose the problem differently in complementary ways?

phases.constraint: State hybrids explicitly and explain what you gain. Don't suggest a hybrid just to seem thorough — only if the combination is genuinely better than either alone.

phases.name: Write the Final Output
phases.description: Follow the output format in SKILL.md exactly.

principles.name: Be direct
principles.rule: Name a winner. Hedging ('it depends') is only acceptable if you provide a concrete decision tree (e.g., 'use X if n < 10^5, use Y otherwise').

principles.name: Show your work
principles.rule: The Analysis section must justify the recommendation, not just restate it.

principles.name: Highlight errors found
principles.rule: If a subagent made a mistake, say so. This is valuable signal for the user that the synthesis step is doing real work.

principles.name: Implementation sketch is mandatory
principles.rule: It doesn't need to be executable, but it must unambiguously communicate the algorithm — pseudocode, commented outline, or annotated steps.

commonMistakesToAvoid.name: Recency bias
commonMistakesToAvoid.description: Don't favor the last path just because you read it last. Score all paths before concluding.

commonMistakesToAvoid.name: Advocacy drift
commonMistakesToAvoid.description: Subagents are briefed to explore one path. Their enthusiasm for their own approach isn't evidence of its quality.

commonMistakesToAvoid.name: False hybrids
commonMistakesToAvoid.description: 'Use both' is not a synthesis. A real hybrid has a specific integration point.

commonMistakesToAvoid.name: Ignoring constraints
commonMistakesToAvoid.description: Re-read the original problem constraints before writing the recommendation. A beautiful O(n log n) solution is wrong if the problem requires O(n).
